coredata.dc:title: Effect of compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent treatment on the synthesis of hierarchically porous nanocarbon from kraft lignin
coredata.dc:creator: Myint, Aye Aye
Seo, Bumjoon
Son, Won-Su
Yoon, Junho
Shin, Naechul
Kim, Jaehoon
Lee, Youn-Woo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanoparticles with hierarchical micro/mesoporous structure were developed from a commercial kraft lignin using a facile, one-pot green technology of a compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent treatment, followed by thermostablization and carbonization processes. The as-synthesized carbon nanoparticles were systematically characterized by investigating the surface properties of morphology, particle size, and chemical states with FESEM, HRTEM and XPS, respectively; surface areas and porous structures with BET analyzer; crystalline state with XRD and Raman spectroscopy; chemical features with FTIR; and elemental compositions with an elemental analyzer. The results indicated that the carbon nanoparticles fabricated from the smallest quasi-spherical lignin nanoparticles contained high nanocrystalline and amorphous carbons levels. The enhanced phenolic, carbonyl, and carboxylic functional groups on the particle surface promoted the dispersibility of the particles in deionized water. The size and morphology of the lignin precursor particles significantly affected the fabrication of the carbon nanoparticles by carbonization. The experiment suggested that such dispersible and porous carbon nanoparticles could be applicable in the fields of energy, biotechnology, and environmental pollution control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Kraft lignin
Compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent
Lignin nanoparticles
Carbonization
Porous carbon nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Lignin nanoparticles preparation using clCO2 antisolvent method
Carbon nanoparticles preparation from the as-synthesized lignin nanoparticles
Characteristics of the synthesized lignin nanoparticles
Characteristics of the carbonized lignin particles
Surface area and pore size distribution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the processed lignins obtained under various experimental temperatures of 280.2K, 288.2K and 298.2K (denoted as PKL-7, PKL-15 and PKL-25, respectively) with the clCO2 antisolvent, A, C and D. The insets in A show the particle size distribution of PKL-7 measured from the TEM image in A, a; and a photograph of a stable colloidal suspension of PKL-7 in DI water at room temperature, b. B depicts HRTEM image of PKL-7 and the inset in B illustrates the corresponding 2D fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of the circled region.

TEM images of the various carbonized particles obtained from the starting materials of the various processed lignin nanoparticles of PKL-7, PKL-15, and PKL-25 (denoted as CPKL-7, CPKL-15, and CPKL-25, respectively), A, C and D. The insets in A illustrate the particle size distribution of CPKL-7 measured from the TEM image, a; and a photograph of a stable colloidal suspension of CPKL-7 in DI water at room temperature, b. B shows HRTEM imaging of CPKL-7. The inset in B displays the corresponding 2D FFT pattern of CPKL-7 from the circled region in B. TEM images of the raw lignin, denoted as CKL-raw, E and F. FESEM images of CKL-raw and CPKL-7 deposited on silicon wafers, G and H, respectively.

Nitrogen sorption isotherms of carbonized particles obtained from the starting materials of raw lignin (denoted as CKL-raw) and various processed lignin nanoparticles of PKL-7, PKL-15, and PKL-25 (denoted as CPKL-7, CPKL-15 and CPKL-25, respectively), A (closed symbols: adsorption, open symbols: desorption); and pore size distribution of CPKL-7, and CPKL-15, and CPKL-25 obtained from the BJH desorption branches, B.

XRD patters, A; Raman spectra, B; and FTIR spectra, C, of carbonized lignin samples obtained from raw lignin and processed lignin nanoparticles of PKL-7 (denoted as CKL-raw and CPKL-7, respectively). sOH : OH stretching of phenolic and aliphatic moieties; sCO : CO stretching; δipOH : OH in-plane deformation of phenolic OH; δCO : CO deformation in secondary alcohols and aliphatic ether; and δopArCH : aromatic CH out-of-plane deformation of the guaiacyl unit.

XPS survey spectra of carbonized lignin samples obtained from raw lignin and processed lignin nanoparticles of PKL-7 (denoted as CKL-raw and CPKL-7, respectively), A; and respective core-level XPS spectra of C 1s, B and C; and O 1s, D and E.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CKL-raw is the carbonized particles obtained from raw kraft lignin (KL-raw); and CPKL-7, CPKL-15 and CPKL-25 are the carbonized particles obtained from processed lignins of PKL-7, PKL-15 and PKL-25, respectively.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Single point adsorption total pore volume.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery yields a (YR ) and elemental compositions b for raw, processed, and carbonized lignin particles.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PKL-7 is the processed lignin obtained at 280.2K; and CKL-raw and CPKL-7 are carbonized particles obtained from raw kraft lignin (KL-raw) and processed lignin (PKL-7), respectively.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Based on 100g of initial raw kraft lignin (KL-raw), w/w%.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Obtained by subtracting the sum weight concentration in percentage of C, H, O, N, and S from 100.

head.ce:title: Effect of compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent treatment on the synthesis of hierarchically porous nanocarbon from kraft lignin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering, College of Engineering and Institute of Chemical Processes (ICP), Seoul National University, 1 Gwanak-ro, Gwanak-gu, Daehak-dong, 151-744 Seoul, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering
College of Engineering and Institute of Chemical Processes (ICP)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Inha University, 253 Yonghyun-dong, Nam-gu, 402-751 Incheon, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, SKKU Advanced Institute of Nanotechnology, Sungkyunkwan University, 2066 Seobu-ro, Jangan-gu, 440-746 Suwon, Gyeonggi-do, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticles with hierarchical micro/mesoporous structure were developed from a commercial kraft lignin using a facile, one-pot green technology of a compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent treatment, followed by thermostablization and carbonization processes. The as-synthesized carbon nanoparticles were systematically characterized by investigating the surface properties of morphology, particle size, and chemical states with FESEM, HRTEM and XPS, respectively; surface areas and porous structures with BET analyzer; crystalline state with XRD and Raman spectroscopy; chemical features with FTIR; and elemental compositions with an elemental analyzer. The results indicated that the carbon nanoparticles fabricated from the smallest quasi-spherical lignin nanoparticles contained high nanocrystalline and amorphous carbons levels. The enhanced phenolic, carbonyl, and carboxylic functional groups on the particle surface promoted the dispersibility of the particles in deionized water. The size and morphology of the lignin precursor particles significantly affected the fabrication of the carbon nanoparticles by carbonization. The experiment suggested that such dispersible and porous carbon nanoparticles could be applicable in the fields of energy, biotechnology, and environmental pollution control.

ce:section.ce:para: Hierarchically porous carbons have recently attracted great attention owing to their promising uses in diverse fields such as energy, environment, or biomedicine [1–5]. Meanwhile, the depletion of non-renewable fossil resources, growing demand and excessive consumption of fossil fuels, and associated global environmental concerns (i.e. environmental pollution and global warming) have motivated extensive research on the development of sustainable new hierarchically porous carbons from cost-effective, environmentally friendly, readily scalable, renewable, and naturally abundant resources, such as biomass [6–8]. As the largest natural source of aromatic biopolymers on earth and a major component of lignocellulosic biomass, lignin is attracting global interest as a precursor for the preparation of bulk chemicals and advanced carbon materials [9–12]. It is also low in cost and readily available as a waste product of the paper and lignocellulosic bioethanol production industries. The efficient valorization of industrial lignin may offer many green benefits by using the material in inexpensive, sustainable, biocompatible, and environmentally friendly products; preventing environmental pollution from the direct burning of industrial lignin to generate energy and the discharge of pollution to local environments; and establishing an economically viable biorefinery with environmental sustainability. Therefore, recent reports have documented various strategies to explore hierarchically porous carbons from various industrial lignin sources including hard-templating, soft-templating, or sol-gel methods [13–15]. Despite recent advances in controlling the porosity of carbon materials through these methods, some critical issues remain for their industrial applications, such as the inclusion of complex multiple-step synthesis processes or toxic chemicals and the requirement for additional post-processing steps to remove template materials. These aspects are typically time-consuming and high in cost, which do not serve the urgent need to save energy and protect the environment. In addition, the selection of a precursor is critical for both controlling the morphology and obtaining desirable porosities in the final carbons [16,17]. Hence, the development of an efficient, environmentally friendly, and more economical synthesis protocol for the preparation of nanostructured carbons with interconnected, hierarchical pore networks using natural precursors is of great significance because of the large-scale production and broad applicability of these materials.

In recent years, significant attention has been paid to the use of high pressure/compressed/supercritical CO2 based technology as an emerging green technology in the diverse fields of biomass processing [18,19] as well as the synthesis of polymeric nanoparticles with controlled morphologies, sizes and size distribution [20]. As high pressure CO2 is a green and sustainable solvent/antisolvent, it is widely used in numerous applications of biomass processing such as pretreatment [21,22], fractionation [23], hydrolysis [24–27] and catalytic conversion [28,29] due to its easily achievable of critical temperature (Tc =304.3K) and pressure (Pc =7.4MPa), inexpensiveness, non-toxicity, non-flammability and environmentally benignity; it is highly soluble in numerous organic solvents and readily recovered/recycled after processing. Besides, due to its low polarizability per unit volume and low cohesive energy, CO2 is a poor solvent for many macromolecules such as polymers and proteins, and different antisolvent methods [30,31] have been emerging based on a similar principle as liquid antisolvent precipitation.

In the present work, we report a facile and versatile route to prepare hierarchically porous carbon nanoparticles using commercial kraft lignin as a renewable feedstock. The route takes a two-step approach: (1) preparing nanoscale lignin particles using a compressed liquid CO2 (clCO2) antisolvent method at different temperatures as described in our previous report [20], and (2) subjecting the as-synthesized lignin nanoparticles to thermostablization and carbonization processes under specified conditions, in order to elucidate the effect of various sizes and morphologies of lignin nanoparticle precursors on the formation of carbon nanoparticles. In preparing the lignin nanoparticles, the influences of various processing temperatures on the product yields, morphologies, size and size distributions, surface areas, and textural features were investigated. The quality of the obtained carbon nanoparticles was elucidated by characterizing the morphologies, textural properties, crystalline states, chemical structures, surface features, and elemental compositions of the products. For comparison, thermostablization and carbonization processes were performed on the raw lignin under the same experimental conditions applied to the nanoparticulate lignin precursors. To our knowledge, no previous work has investigated the influence of clCO2 antisolvent treatment on the synthesis of hierarchically porous nanocarbon from industrial lignin. This method may promote the development of porous carbon nanoparticles for many potential applications.

N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%) was obtained from Samchun Pure Chemical Co. Ltd., Korea. Carbon dioxide (CO2, 99.99%) and nitrogen (N2, 99.999%) were supplied by HyupShin Gas Industry Co. Ltd., Korea. Kraft (alkali) lignin was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA, and used as received. Deionized (DI) water was prepared by a Milli-Q water-purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lignin nanoparticles preparation using clCO2 antisolvent method
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed configuration of the apparatus and experimental procedures to obtain lignin nanoparticles has been described in our previous report [20]. Briefly, the CO2 was initially delivered by pumping at a specific flow rate to the precipitator through a cooler at 258.2K to ensure the liquefaction of the gas to prevent cavitation. After the desired operating temperature and pressure of the precipitating vessel were stabilized, the prepared lignin solution was sprayed into the precipitator at the desired flow rate via a nozzle (inside diameter: 254μm). The precipitated particles were collected on membrane filter paper (pore size: 0.45μm, PTFE, Millipore, Italy) in the filter which was made of SUS316. After all the solution was supplied, the products in the filter was washed by continuously pumping with only CO2 for 30min to remove any residual solvent entrapped in the precipitated lignin particles. After the washing step, the filter and precipitator were depressurized, and the filter was separated from the apparatus. From the separated filter, the particles were taken out and weighed to measure the recovery yield. The percentage of recovery of the precipitated lignin particles (YRforPKL ) was calculated by the quantity of the collected particles on the filter paper (g) divided by the quantity of the initial raw lignin (g) multiplied by 100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanoparticles preparation from the as-synthesized lignin nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The as-synthesized lignin nanoparticles (2g) were thermostablized at 250°C with a ramp rate of 1°Cmin−1 for holding 2h under an air atmosphere in a muffle furnace (Thermo Scientific® FD 1535M, USA) to remove any volatile matters and prevent sticking, shrinking, melting of lignin nanoparticles during sequent carbonization [32,33]. Carbonization was performed on the thermostabilized samples in a vacuum tube furnace (Vacuum Science Laboratory, Korea) at 700°C with a ramp rate of 5°Cmin−1 and held for 2h under a N2 atmosphere to produce amorphous carbons, while most benzene rings structure were transformed into aromatic structures with emission of CO [34]. The thermostablization and carbonization processes were also carried out for the raw lignin under the same operating conditions in order to compare with the features of the carbonized lignins obtained from the processed lignin nanoparticles as the starting materials. The percentages of overall recovery yields of the carbonized lignin particles were calculated using the following Eqs. (1) and (2):

For the processed lignin nanoparticles as starting materials—
(1)
Y
R f o r C P K L

m

T P K L
0

R f o r P K L

For the raw lignin as a starting material—
(2)
Y
R f o r C K L
[ % ]

m

× 100
where,
Y
R f o r C P K L
and
Y
R f o r C K L
are the respective yields in percents of the carbonized particles from the processed lignin nanoparticles and raw lignin;
m
C P K L
and
m
C K L
are the respective mass in grams of the processed lignin nanoparticles and raw lignin after carbonization; and
m
T P K L
0
and
m
T K L
0
are the respective mass in grams of the processed lignin nanoparticles and raw lignin before thermostablization.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were used to examine the shape and size of raw, processed and carbonized lignin particles. The FESEM images were acquired on a SURRA 55VP FE-SEM (Carl Zeiss, Germany) operated at 2kV and various resolving powers after mounting lignin particles deposited double sided adhesive carbon tape and carbon particles deposited silicon wafer onto aluminum specimen stubs, and sputter coating with 10nm platinum using a BAL-TEC/SCD 005 sputter coater (SoftComp, Germany). The TEM and HRTEM images were acquired on a JEM 3010 HRTEM (JEOL, Japan) operated at an acceleration voltage of 300kV with a GATAN Ultrascan CCD Camera after drop-casting the suspended samples in DI water onto super-thin carbon film coated 300mesh copper grids (Electron Microscopy Sciences, USA) and air drying the grids for overnight. The particle size distributions of the processed and carbonized lignin particles were measured from the FESEM and TEM images using ImageJ software [35]. The histogram of particle sizes obtained by FESEM and TEM images over 300 particles was fitted by a Gaussian function. A particle size analyzer Analysette 22 (MicroTech Plus, Fritsch, Germany) was used to measure the particle size and size distribution of the raw lignin. The porosity parameters of the raw, processed and carbonized lignin particles were determined using an ASAP 2020 (Accelerated Surface Area and Porosimetry) instrument (Micromeritics, USA). Prior to sorption measurements, degassing of the raw and processed lignin particles was performed at 333.2K for 4h whereas that of the carbonized samples was conducted at 573.2K for 2h in a vacuum chamber. The total surface area, SBET, was calculated by the multipoint BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) method [36]. The surface areas of micropore, Smic and mesopore, Smeso ; and the micropore volume (pore size <2nm), Vmic , were obtained from the t-plot method, based on the representation of the isotherm versus the thickness of the adsorbed layer on a non-porous reference material [37]. The total pore volume, VTot , was estimated from the amount adsorbed at a relative pressure (P P0 −1) =0.98 [38]. The pore size distributions of the carbonized lignin particles were estimated by the BJH (Barrett–Joyner–Halenda) method from desorption branches.

The crystal phases of the carbonized samples were investigated by using a Rigaku Miniflex X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ=0.15406nm) operated at 40kV and 200mA. Diffraction patterns were recorded using a 2θ range of 10–60° with a step size of 0.02°. The Raman spectra of the carbonized samples on silicon wafers were examined by using a LabRAM HR 800 micro-Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA) equipped with an Alpha300R confocal Raman microscope (WITec, USA) through a 100 × objective lens. Raman spectra were recorded at 532nm laser excitation wavelength with 50mW power; and the Raman spectrum of each sample corresponds to the average of all the spectra (n≥3) in each image. The chemical features of the carbonized samples were determined using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). For the FTIR analysis, a small amount of sample was taken to prepare 1% KBr pellet and the spectrum was obtained by averaging 32 scans from 4000 to 400cm−1 at 4cm−1 resolution for each background and sample spectrum, respectively. The chemical states of the component elements were examined using an AXIS-HSi XPS instrument (Kratos Analytical Ltd., Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with an Mg Kα X-ray source operated at 150W and a charge neutralizer. Spectra were recorded using an analyzer pass energy of 20eV and a step size of 0.1eV per step. The quantification was performed using the default relative sensitivity factor (RSF) values supplied by the XPS manufacturer. Data analysis was performed using CasaXPS software. Two-point energy stable referencing was made using adventitious C (284.5eV) and valance bond energy corrections. The elemental contents of the raw, processed and carbonized lignin particles were characterized by using a CHNS/O analyzer (ThermoFinnigan Flash EA 1112, Thermo Electron, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the synthesized lignin nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the effect of process temperature on the characteristics of particles formed in the clCO2 antisolvent using the particles by a compressed fluid antisolvent (PCA) process [20], experiments were performed at varied process temperatures of 280.2K, 288.2K and 298.2K while keeping constant for other operational parameters: process pressure of 15.0MPa, initial lignin concentration of 100mgmL−1, solution flow rate of 0.06kgh−1 and CO2 flow rate of 3.0kgh−1. Hereafter, the processed lignins obtained at temperatures of 280.2K, 288.2K and 298.2K are denoted as PKL-7, PKL-15 and PKL-25, respectively; and the raw lignin is denoted as KL-raw.

The FESEM images of the processed lignins obtained from various process temperatures with the clCO2 antisolvents; and their corresponding mean particle diameters with standard deviations and size distributions are exhibited in Fig. 1 (A–C) and (D–F), respectively. To provide a comparison with the processed lignin samples, raw lignin was analyzed as received using FESEM and a wet particle analyzer with results obtained is illustrated in the Supplementary data, Fig. S1(A and B). As a result, relatively small nanoparticles with defined quasi-spherical shapes, PKL-7 (depicted in Fig. 1A), were achieved when the reaction was performed at a low temperature of 280.2K. As indicated in Fig. 1D, the mean diameter of these particles is approximately 55nm with a narrow size distribution peak. In contrast, the raw lignin, presented in the Supplementary data of Fig. S1(A and B), comprises irregularly shaped microparticles ranging widely between 29.7 and 69.9μm in size.

The TEM results in Fig. 2 reveal that the various processing temperatures have substantial influence on the morphology and size of particles formed using the clCO2 antisolvent, which is consistent with the previous FESEM morphological characterization. Strikingly, the TEM images of PKL-7 in Fig. 2A depict uniform structures with monodisperse particles. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2B also shows that the lignin nanoparticles of PKL-7 possess a graphitic-lattice structure having d-spacing of ∼0.334nm and an average primary particle size of 6.0±0.7nm (see inset (a) in Fig. 2A). The particles are well dispersed in deionized (DI) water at room temperature (see inset (b) in Fig. 2A). In contrast, the raw lignin particle is relatively large with an irregular dense and solid structure, as presented in the Supplementary data of Fig. S1(C and D).

The textural properties of the processed lignin particles as determined by nitrogen sorption analysis are presented in Fig. 3 ; the respective nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms are depicted in the Supplementary data of Fig. S2A. For comparison, the raw lignin was also analyzed with results shown in the Supplementary data, Fig. S2B. Interestingly, the influences of operational temperatures on the particle textural characteristics of the processed lignin particles were observed. BET surface area (SBET ) of the processed lignin nanoparticles ranged from 23.5 to 54.8m2 g−1 whereas that of the raw lignin was only ∼0.2m2 g−1. Under the same conditions, SBET , Smeso , and VTot of the lignin nanoparticles decreased with increases in the temperature from 280.2K to 298.2K. Nanoparticles that were smaller and less aggregated had higher surface areas, while larger particles with more aggregation or coalescence showed considerably lower surface areas. For example, PKL-7, with the smallest monodisperse lignin particles of defined quasi-spherical shape, had a high surface area approaching 55m2 g−1. On the contrary, the largest and more aggregated particles of PKL-25 had the lowest surface area of 23.5m2 g−1. Notably, the surface area results agree well with the results obtained from the corresponding FESEM and TEM morphological characterizations. It is also apparent that the Smeso of the lignin nanoparticles estimated by the t-plot method corresponds to the SBET . This further verifies that the porosity of the processed lignin particles is a result of the mesopores, which is consistent with the results of our previous report [20] regardless of differences in initial concentrations of the lignin solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the carbonized lignin particles
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we performed thermostablization and carbonization processes on the three processed lignins of PKL-7, PKL-15 and PKL-25, in order to investigate the effect of nanoparticle size and textural properties of different precursors on the quality of the resulting carbonized particles. For comparison, the same experiments were performed on the raw lignin under the same operational conditions applied to the processed lignin nanoparticles precursors. Hereafter, the carbonized lignins obtained from the raw lignin is denoted as CKL-raw; and various processed lignin nanoparticles of PKL-7, PKL-15 and PKL-25 are denoted as CPKL-7, CPKL-15 and CPKL-25, respectively.

Fig. 4 depicts TEM images of the carbonized lignin particles fabricated from PKL-7, PKL-15, and PKL-25, shown in Fig. 4(A, C, D); and particles of carbonized raw lignin, CKL-raw, are depicted at different magnifications in Fig. 4(E and F). The variously sized lignin nanoparticle precursors yield various nano-sized carbonized particles; meanwhile, raw lignin produces a large and heterogeneous solid mass of carbonized particles after thermostablization and carbonization. In particular, smaller lignin nanoparticle precursors yield smaller carbon nanoparticles than larger precursors do. This highlights the importance of the size and morphology of the precursors in the fabrication of carbon nanoparticles by pyrolysis. The smallest monodisperse nano-sized carbon particles with an average diameter of 3.9nm (inset (a) in Fig. 4A) are achieved from the smallest defined quasi-spherical PKL-7 lignin nanoparticles. The final particles are dispersible in DI water (inset (b) in Fig. 4A) and, as shown in the HRTEM image in Fig. 4B, have graphitic-like lattice fringes with interfacial spacing of ∼0.287nm, slightly larger than the lattice spacing of graphene at ∼0.24nm [39]. Nanocrystalline domains of the CPKL-7 particles are also verified by the 2D fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern [40,41], presented as the inset of Fig. 4B. Meanwhile, the FESEM image in Fig. 4H reveals a CPKL-7 surface that is randomly rough at the microscale for the quasi-spherical nanoparticle clusters, which are aggregated to form a porous structure [42]. In contrast, the FESEM image of CKL-raw shows relatively large and irregular microparticles (Fig. 4G), which is consistent with the TEM image analysis results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface area and pore size distribution
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 5 A shows the nitrogen sorption isotherms for four carbonized lignins, with respective surface areas and pore properties summarized in Table 1 . According to IUPAC classifications [38], the nitrogen sorption isotherm of CKL-raw is type I, typical to microporous structures, while the other samples of CPKL-7, CPKL-15, and CPKL-25 follow type II nitrogen sorption isotherms with typical H4 hysteresis loops. These are generally observed in complex materials containing both micropores and mesopores [43,44]. Notably, Table 1 shows that CPKL-7 has a very high surface area (SBET
≈606m2 g−1) and total pore volume (VTot
≈0.29cm3 g−1); these are higher by factors of ∼2.5 and ∼2.8 than those of CKL-raw, respectively, with high contributions from the micropore and mesopore fractions (Smic
≈533m2 g−1 and Vmeso
≈0.046cm3 g−1). As depicted in Fig. 5B, the pore size distributions for CPKL-7, CPKL-15, and CPKL-25, obtained by the BJH model from the desorption branches, further confirms the presence of micropores and mesopores in the carbons. Noteworthily, the interconnected pore networks in the hierarchically porous structures of these three carbons (micropores <2nm and mesopores ∼2.7–4.4nm), derived from the processed lignin nanoparticles, may make these materials attractive for applications in advanced technology, including electrochemical supercapacitors, Li batteries, solar cells, fuel cells, catalyst supports, and sorbents for toxic gas separation [1,3,4]. The results of pore structural analysis agree well with the previous results of morphological characterization.

The crystal features of the carbonized products were investigated by XRD and Raman spectroscopy. In Fig. 6 A and the Supplementary data of Fig. S3, the XRD patterns of all carbonized products show two broad peaks positioned at 2θ values of ∼22–24° and ∼42–44°, attributed to the typical graphitic (002) and (100) planes, respectively [45]. The breadth of the peaks is attributed to the amorphous nature of the carbons [46,47] and indicates the low degree of graphitization [48–50]. However, various degrees of peak intensity are observed: CPKL-7<CPKL-15<CPKL-25<CKL-raw (Fig. S3). The low intensity and broad amorphous region centered at 2θ=23.3° for CPKL-7 indicates the small crystal size and low crystalline content of the amorphous carbon nanoparticles. The coexistence of nanocrystalline and amorphous carbon domains in CPKL-7 is verified by Raman analysis; the spectrum exhibits two equally intense and broad D and G bands at ∼1344cm−1 and 1589cm−1, respectively [48,51], with a modulated bump at ∼2400–3250cm−1 attributed to the 2D region (Fig. 6B). By contrast, CKL-raw has low-intensity D and G bands and a flat 2D region. The D band arises from the disordered crystalline structure in the sp2 clusters, while the G band is assigned to the in-plane stretching vibration mode E2g of single-crystalline graphite [52]. The 2D band, originating from a two-photon double-resonance Raman process [53], is referred to as the G′ band [54], which can be assigned to sp3 CHx stretching [55]. The peculiar 2D band shape in the Raman spectrum of CPKL-7 is probably a result of the chemical functionalization of the graphene basal plane from localized physical defects in the sp2 -conjugated carbon [56,57].

The chemical structural and surface features of the carbonized lignin particles were elucidated by FTIR spectroscopy and XPS. As seen in Fig. 6C and the Supplementary data of Fig. S4, the FTIR spectrum of CKL-raw exhibits the chemical structure of amorphous carbon derived by the pyrolysis of lignin reported in the literature [40,58], including the OH stretching band (maximum at ∼3442cm−1) probably caused by the decomposition of phenolic hydroxyls [59], CC stretching vibrations in aromatic structures (at ∼1635cm−1), OH stretching of phenolic and aliphatic moieties (at ∼1385cm−1), CO deformation in secondary alcohols and aliphatic ether (at ∼1048–∼1117cm−1), and CH out-of-plane deformation of aromatics (at ∼876cm−1). The spectra of CPKL-7, CPKL-15, and CPKL-25 shown in Fig. S4 contain similar characteristic absorption bands to those seen in the spectrum of CKL-raw, but with somewhat different intensities, suggesting that the morphology, size, and size distribution of the precursors may influence the chemical structure of the resulting carbons. Intense characteristic bands of OH at ∼3444cm−1 and ∼1385cm−1 and of CO at ∼1113cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of the CPKL-7 indicate much higher contents of phenolic hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups than that indicated by the bands of the CKL-raw. These functional groups are responsible for the enhanced dispersion stability of the carbon nanoparticles (inset (b) in Fig. 4A).

Meanwhile, the XPS survey spectrum of CPKL-7 shows the presence of a core level O 1s more intense than that of C 1s, while the reverse core level intensity is observed in the spectrum for CKL-raw (Fig. 7 A), suggesting that a lower degree of graphitization occurs for CPKL-7 compared to that for CKL-raw, which is consistent with the XRD results. The core level spectra of C 1s and O 1s with the fitting data for CKL-raw and CPKL-7 are also presented in Fig. 7(B–E). The relative compositions of surface functional groups, obtained by the binding energies of the component peaks in the C 1s and O 1s signals, are summarized in the Supplementary data of Table S1. In the C 1s spectrum, multiple peak contributions are evident from the extended and asymmetric shape, while only two oxygen component peaks are resolved in the O 1s spectra. These can be deconvoluted, as previously described, to extract information on the sp2 and sp3 carbon bonding percentages and the concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups [60–63]. As seen in Fig. 7(B and C), the main C 1s peaks for CKL-raw and CPKL-7 demonstrate substantially different contributions of the sp2 − (at 284.5eV) and sp3 − (at 285.5eV) bonded carbon atoms, with sp2 /sp3 bond ratios of 16.97 for CKL-raw and 1.63 for CPKL-7 (Table S1). In addition, the CPKL-7 has higher contents of COC, CO, and OCOH groups relative to CKL-raw. This confirms that the CPKL-7 has a high concentration of amorphous carbon with oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, which agrees well with the FTIR and Raman results.

Table 2 summarizes the yields and elemental compositions of the processed lignin nanoparticles (PKL-7) and carbonized lignin particles fabricated from the raw and processed lignin precursors (CKL-raw and CPKL-7, respectively), as compared to that of the raw lignin (KL-raw). In order to see the changes in color through the preparation process, photographs of the raw, processed, thermostablized, and carbonized lignins are displayed in the Supplementary data of Fig. S5. The overall yields for CKL-raw and CPKL-7 are similar at ∼36% and ∼33%, respectively, and slightly lower than the reported values [56,62] of ∼40%. Interestingly, Sharma et al. [64] postulated that the high char yields might be caused by inorganic components in lignin, which is supported by the results of the present study. The results of elemental analysis show that KL-raw consists of ∼61.7% C, ∼5.6% H, ∼25.3% O, ∼0.5% N, ∼1.4% S, and ∼5.6% of other materials. From this, the contents of water and ash, which mainly consists of inorganic matters like oxides of sodium, potassium, silicon, and aluminum derived from the sources and processing steps of the raw biomass, can be assumed [65]. A higher O/C ratio and lower concentration of other materials are found for PKL-7 as compared to KL-raw. Consequently, CPKL-7 contains higher C/H and O/C ratios and a lower concentration of other matter, whereas CKL-raw has the most N, S, and other elements. These analyses agree well with the XPS results.

A new facile and versatile method to synthesize hierarchically porous carbon nanoparticles with high surface areas and pore volumes using the commercial waste product of kraft lignin as a precursor is presented. The synthesized carbon nanoparticles obtained from the smallest quasi-spherical lignin nanoparticles contained high levels of nanocrystalline and amorphous carbons with enhanced oxygen-containing functional groups (i.e., COC, CO, and OCOH) on the particle surfaces; these showed good dispersibility in DI water. The results of this study highlight that the size and morphology of the lignin precursor are important in the fabrication of carbon nanoparticles by carbonization. In addition, the PCA method is versatile, industrially scalable, and environmentally friendly. Undoubtedly, the merit properties (high surface area, small crystalline size, and functional groups) of the fabricated dispersible and hierarchically porous carbon nanoparticles may be attractive candidate as catalyst supports in many potential applications (e.g. biodiesel synthesis, biomass transformation and so on).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE), Republic of Korea; and Brain Korea 21 Program for Leading Universities and Students (BK21 PLUS) at the school of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Seoul National University, Republic of Korea.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.supflu.2016.12.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
